title: SEGMENT 2 — Carl Woese and the universal tree: the 16S revolution

lines.text: SEGMENT 2 — Carl Woese and the universal tree: the 16S revolution
Before Norman Pace pulled DNA out of an environmental sample, somebody had to figure out which DNA to pull.

The whole subsequent field of culture-free microbial ecology depends on a single insight that came out of a remarkable, isolated, somewhat eccentric career in the American Midwest: Carl Woese, at the University of Illinois, working through the 1970s, on a question that almost nobody else was asking.
The question was deceptively simple.
How are all living things related to one another?
Darwin had drawn the famous tree of life sketch in his notebook in 1837 — that little branching diagram with the words "I think" above it — and ever since, biologists had tried to fill in the tree.
For animals and plants, you could do reasonably well by comparing anatomy and embryology.
But for microbes — bacteria — you couldn't.
Bacteria are tiny featureless rods or spheres or spirals; they don't have skeletons or wing patterns.
Classical bacterial taxonomy was based on what they ate, how they stained, what shape they were, and what diseases they caused.
None of those properties tell you reliably about evolutionary relationships.
Two bacteria that look identical under a microscope might be as distantly related as you are to a mushroom.
Woese decided to take a completely different approach: comparing sequences of a single molecule that every living thing has.

If you could find a gene that was universal — present in every cell on Earth — and that evolved slowly enough that you could still align sequences from very different organisms, then differences in that gene's sequence would directly measure evolutionary distance.
Lots of changes between two organisms in this molecule, distantly related.
Few changes, closely related.
A universal molecular clock.
The molecule Woese chose was ribosomal RNA.
Specifically, in bacteria and archaea, the small subunit of the ribosome contains a chunk of RNA about 1,500 nucleotides long, called the 16S rRNA.
In eukaryotes — that's us, plus fungi, plants, animals, all the things with nuclei — the equivalent molecule in the small subunit is called the 18S rRNA.
They're homologous; they do the same job; they evolved from the same ancestral molecule billions of years ago.

Ribosomal RNA is universal because every cell on Earth needs ribosomes to make proteins, and the ribosome is so central to life that its core RNA components have been conserved with remarkably little change since the last universal common ancestor.
Here is the analogy.
Imagine you're trying to figure out the family tree of every human being on Earth, and the only piece of evidence you have is a single sentence that everyone has written down somewhere.
That sentence has been passed from parent to child for thousands of generations, copied by hand each time, accumulating small errors at each copy.
People in the same family will have nearly identical versions of the sentence.
People from distant lineages will have versions that have drifted apart, with many differences.
Compare the sentences, count the differences, and you can reconstruct the family tree.
That's exactly what Woese was doing with 16S rRNA.
The sentence had been copied for four billion years.
The errors were the substitutions, insertions, and deletions that accumulated in the RNA sequence over evolutionary time.
By sequencing 16S from many different bacteria and aligning the sequences, Woese could calculate how related any two organisms were, in a way that made no assumption about morphology or physiology.
The 16S molecule had two more crucial properties that made it perfect for this.
First, it has a mix of highly conserved regions and highly variable regions.

The conserved regions, which form the structural core of the ribosome, are essentially identical across all of life — these you can use to align sequences from organisms as different as E. coli and a human.
The variable regions, between the conserved blocks, accumulate mutations faster and are different even between closely related species — these are where you read the actual phylogenetic signal.
The molecule has, built into it, both an alignment ruler and a discrimination ruler.

Second, ribosomal RNA is present in every cell at very high copy number — there are tens of thousands of ribosomes in every bacterial cell — which made it experimentally tractable to isolate enough material to sequence in the era before PCR existed.
In 1977, Woese and his collaborator George Fox published what would turn out to be one of the most important papers in modern biology, almost completely ignored at the time.
They had been sequencing 16S rRNA from a strange group of microbes called the methanogens — single-celled organisms that produce methane, found in swamps and the guts of cows and the deep ocean.
The methanogens had been classified for decades as a kind of unusual bacterium.
But when Woese compared their 16S sequences to those of normal bacteria and to those of eukaryotes, he discovered something shocking.
The methanogens were not bacteria at all.
Their 16S sequences were as different from regular bacteria as bacteria were from eukaryotes.
They formed a completely separate third branch of life, equal in evolutionary depth to the other two.
Woese named this third domain the Archaebacteria, later shortened to Archaea.
The classical two-domain view of life — prokaryotes versus eukaryotes — was wrong.
The real picture was three domains: Bacteria, Archaea, and Eukarya.

The methanogens, plus a bunch of other organisms we now classify as Archaea — the extreme thermophiles that live near hydrothermal vents, the halophiles that live in concentrated brine, and many others — represent a vast and ancient branch of life that microscopy and culture-based microbiology had been quietly lumping together with bacteria for a century.
They look like bacteria.
They're single cells without nuclei.
But their cellular biochemistry — membrane lipids, gene expression machinery, replication enzymes — is in many ways closer to ours than to the bacteria's.
This was a foundational discovery, and it was greeted with extraordinary skepticism at the time.
Senior microbiologists were furious.
The textbooks had to be rewritten.
Woese, who was already considered slightly difficult and obsessive, was further marginalized.
It took nearly a decade for the three-domain tree to become widely accepted.

He was eventually given the Crafoord Prize and the National Medal of Science but never the Nobel, partly because, I think, the establishment never fully forgave him for being right about something so fundamental that the textbooks had to change.
For our purposes today, what matters is the methodological legacy.
Woese's work established that 16S rRNA is the universal phylogenetic marker.
Every bacterium and archaeon has it.
The sequence carries an evolutionary signal that lets you place any organism on the tree of life by reading a few hundred nucleotides.
And — here is the key insight that Norman Pace would build on a few years later — you don't need to grow the organism to sequence its 16S.
All you need is its DNA.
If you can pull DNA out of an environmental sample and amplify the 16S gene from it, you can identify the organism without ever culturing it.
The 16S is a barcode, written into every cell, that tells you who the cell is.
Read the barcode, you've identified the bug.
That's the universal-barcode framing I want you to hold.
The 16S is a slowly evolving, universally present, internally variable RNA gene whose sequence is a fingerprint of phylogenetic identity.
Carl Woese figured this out by hand-sequencing RNA in the 1970s — a brutally laborious process.
Norman Pace, in the next segment, will take Woese's insight and combine it with PCR to do what was previously impossible.
